normative_source.standard_name: OWASP Mobile Application Security Verification Standard
normative_source.requirement_title: The app performs identity pinning for all remote endpoints under the developer's control.
normative_source.normative_excerpt: Per OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 §MASVS-NETWORK-2 (Description): Instead of trusting all the default root CAs of the framework or device, this control will make sure that only very specific CAs are trusted. This practice is typically called certificate pinning or public key pinning.
normative_source.normative_summary: Mobile apps must restrict trust on connections to developer-controlled remote endpoints by pinning the expected certificate, certificate chain, or public key — instead of relying on the platform's default root CA store. Pinning narrows the TLS trust boundary from "any CA the platform trusts" to "the specific keys this app's backend uses," defeating attacks that compromise any CA in the platform's default trust store. Pinning must include backup pin discipline (to support key rotation without bricking the app), an explicit pin-rotation policy, and graceful failure handling.

source_rights.license_notes: Source license recorded from the downloaded OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 PDF artifact. Live website may differ; this corpus pins the artifact license. Verbatim excerpts include attribution per CC-BY-SA-4.0 §3.

applicability.human_note: Applies at Tier 4+ per Constitution Pillar 5 (Secure Architecture & Runtime). SEC-PILLAR-5-CERTIFICATE-PINNING is a Tier 4-5 control because identity pinning becomes a hard requirement at high-assurance tiers (financial, regulated, enterprise-grade apps) where the cost of a CA compromise outweighs the operational burden of pin rotation. At Tier 1-3, MASVS-NETWORK-1's baseline TLS discipline (platform-default trust store with secure-transport enforcement) is sufficient; pinning is optional and the operational risk of bricking via pin staleness exceeds the marginal security gain. The blueprint schema does not declare a discrete network-presence field; the tier-4-floor applicability is enforced via the app_tier check. In practice every Intrinsic Code Tier 4+ app performs network communication with developer-controlled backends because cloud sync, authentication, telemetry, or content delivery are baseline.

intrinsic_interpretation.mapping_rationale: Constitution Pillar 5's SEC-PILLAR-5-CERTIFICATE-PINNING trace and MASVS-NETWORK-2 describe the same control: identity pinning for remote endpoints under the developer's control. Both restrict TLS trust to a developer-managed pin set rather than the platform default trust store; both apply to developer-controlled endpoints specifically (not to third-party flows the developer doesn't operate). The mapping is direct because the trace and the MASVS control are co-extensive — pinning is pinning. Implementation correctness for pin selection (leaf vs intermediate vs SPKI hash), backup pin discipline (to support key rotation), pin expiry handling, and graceful failure modes requires auditor judgment beyond manifest declaration. The mapping is direct at the control-statement level, but the auditor evaluation chain (file_standards_auditor + reaper-mobile + reaper-dependencies) is necessary for full evaluation of correctness.

expected_evidence_patterns: iOS: certificate pinning configured via URLSessionDelegate URLSession(_:didReceive:completionHandler:) with explicit SecTrust evaluation against pinned SPKI hashes, or via a vetted library (TrustKit, Alamofire ServerTrustPolicy) with declared pin set
Android: certificate pinning configured via Network Security Configuration <pin-set> declarations in res/xml/network_security_config.xml, or via OkHttp CertificatePinner with declared pin set
security_manifest declares pin set per developer-controlled endpoint: hostname → set of SPKI SHA-256 hashes (primary + at least one backup)
Pin rotation policy declared in security_manifest: expected pin-set update cadence, rotation procedure, communication path for forced app update when pin set must change before existing-build expiry
Backup pins included for every pinned endpoint (defense against single-key-loss bricking the app) — at least one backup SPKI hash per endpoint
Pin failure handling declared: connection terminated, error surfaced to telemetry (not silently swallowed), no fallback to unpinned trust
failure_indicators: Pinning declared for some developer-controlled endpoints but not others — partial coverage allows attackers to target the unpinned endpoints
Custom TLS validation that accepts any certificate matching a leaf-cert hash, with no backup pin — a single CA-compromise-driven re-issuance bricks the app permanently
Pin set hardcoded as cleartext strings in source without inclusion in the security_manifest declaration — auditor cannot verify rotation policy or backup-pin discipline
Pin failure silently swallowed (caught exception, connection retried without pinning) — defeats the entire purpose of pinning
Pin set tied to a single intermediate CA without leaf SPKI hash or backup — vulnerable to intermediate CA rotation by the upstream CA without app coordination
Pinning applied to third-party endpoints (analytics SDK backends, ad networks) that the developer does NOT control — those endpoints can rotate keys without notice and brick the app
Pinning bypassed in debug builds without explicit security_manifest declaration of the bypass scope — debug-build bypass surface that ships to production is a regression risk
false_positive_traps: Network calls inside test fixtures or test-only code paths (paths containing /test/, /tests/, /__tests__/, /spec/ OR functions/methods marked with @Test, @Ignore, @Disabled annotations). Test mocks legitimately bypass pinning to point at mock servers; these are not production violations.

Pinning NOT applied to third-party endpoints the developer does not control (analytics SDK callbacks, ad networks, CDN-fronted public assets) is NOT a MASVS-NETWORK-2 violation — the control scopes to "endpoints under the developer's control." Third-party endpoint security is the third party's responsibility.

OS-level platform-owned flows (captive portal detection, system update checks, OCSP/CRL fetches by the TLS stack itself) are not subject to MASVS-NETWORK-2 — the developer does not control those flows. The violation surface is app-controlled flows to developer-controlled endpoints only.

Pin set that includes an intermediate CA hash alongside leaf SPKI hashes is NOT a violation — intermediate pinning is a documented pattern for organizations that rotate leaf certs frequently and want to pin to the issuing CA they operate. The violation surface is intermediate-only pinning without leaf SPKI coverage when the developer DOES rotate keys frequently.

allowed_claims: uses Constitution Pillar 5 certificate-pinning discipline (SEC-PILLAR-5-CERTIFICATE-PINNING) as primary evidence toward MASVS-NETWORK-2 coverage at Tier 4+
pins identity for all developer-controlled remote endpoints with declared backup pin set and rotation policy per MASVS-NETWORK-2 requirement
narrows TLS trust boundary from platform-default root CA store to developer-declared pin set, defeating CA-compromise attacks per MASVS-NETWORK-2 intent
claim_boundaries.claim_boundary_notes: Standard-level baseline (from manifest.json.standard_claim_policy) already covers OWASP-wide forbidden claims. No requirement-specific additions needed for MASVS-NETWORK-2. MASVS-NETWORK-2 is mapped to one Constitution v0.5 trace in mapping file v1.2: SEC-PILLAR-5-CERTIFICATE-PINNING (primary, direct). The mapping is direct because the trace and the MASVS control state the same requirement. The control explicitly scopes to "endpoints under the developer's control" — claims about pinning coverage do not extend to third-party endpoints (analytics SDKs, ad networks, public CDN assets) that the developer does not operate. Claims about defeating CA-compromise attacks are valid only when pinning is correctly implemented with backup pin discipline; pinning to a single key without backup creates a different risk class (operational fragility) that is not a security improvement. MASVS-NETWORK-2 applies only at Tier 4+ per the applicability gate. Tier routing handled downstream by Reaper Conclave matrix; the listed Reaper auditors consume this requirement when invoked for the bundle's tier and surface.
